title: Cloud CI/CD Overview
content: CI/CD is the backbone of modern cloud engineering. It automates the journey from a developer's 'git push' to a live application running in the cloud, ensuring speed, reliability, and consistency.
insight: Automation is the key to scaling your software delivery.

title: What is CI?
content: Continuous Integration is the practice of frequently merging code changes into a central repository. Automated builds and tests are run to detect bugs as early as possible.
insight: CI is about catching errors 'left' (earlier) in the development cycle.

title: What is CD?
content: Continuous Delivery ensures that the code is always in a deployable state. Continuous Deployment goes one step further and automatically deploys every change that passes the tests to production.
insight: CD is about making releases a non-event through automation.

title: The CI/CD Pipeline
content: A pipeline is a series of automated steps that your code passes through. Typical stages include: Source, Build, Test, and Deploy.
insight: A pipeline is the 'assembly line' for your software.

title: Source Stage (Git)
content: The pipeline is triggered when changes are detected in your version control system (like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket).
insight: Git is the 'start button' for your automation.

title: Build Stage
content: In the build stage, your code is compiled (if necessary), dependencies are downloaded, and the application is packaged into an artifact (like a JAR file or a Docker image).
insight: The build stage turns raw code into a 'shippable' package.

title: Test Stage
content: Automated tests (Unit, Integration, Linting) are run to ensure the code meets quality standards and doesn't break existing functionality.
insight: Tests are the 'quality control' of your assembly line.

title: Deploy Stage
content: The final stage where the artifact is moved to the target environment (e.g., pushed to ECS, updated in Lambda, or uploaded to S3).
insight: The deploy stage is when your changes become visible to users.

title: GitHub Actions Overview
content: GitHub Actions is a popular CI/CD tool integrated directly into GitHub. You define your pipelines in YAML files stored in the '.github/workflows' directory.
insight: Build, test, and deploy right where your code lives.

title: Workflows, Jobs, and Steps
content: A 'Workflow' is an automated process. A workflow contains one or more 'Jobs'. A job contains a series of 'Steps' that run in a specific environment (runner).
insight: GitHub Actions structure: Workflow -> Jobs -> Steps.

title: Runners
content: Runners are the servers that execute your GitHub Actions jobs. You can use GitHub-hosted runners (Ubuntu, Windows, macOS) or host your own (self-hosted runners).
insight: Runners are the 'laborers' that do the work of your pipeline.

title: GitHub Secrets
content: Never hardcode API keys or passwords in your YAML files. Store them as 'Secrets' in GitHub and access them in your workflow via environment variables.
insight: Secrets keep your sensitive credentials safe from the public eye.

title: AWS CodePipeline
content: A fully managed continuous delivery service that helps you automate your release pipelines for fast and reliable application and infrastructure updates.
insight: The AWS-native way to build enterprise-grade pipelines.

title: AWS CodeBuild
content: A fully managed build service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces software packages that are ready to deploy.
insight: CodeBuild scales automatically and you only pay for the build minutes you use.

title: The buildspec.yml
content: A collection of build commands and related settings, in YAML format, that CodeBuild uses to run a build.
insight: The 'instruction manual' for your AWS build process.

title: AWS CodeDeploy
content: A fully managed deployment service that automates software deployments to various compute services such as Amazon EC2, AWS Fargate, AWS Lambda, and on-premises servers.
insight: CodeDeploy handles the complex logic of rolling out updates safely.

title: The appspec.yml
content: The Application Specification (AppSpec) file is used by CodeDeploy to manage the deployment of an application to the target environment.
insight: The 'instruction manual' for your AWS deployment process.

title: Pipeline Artifacts
content: Artifacts are the files or data produced by a stage and used by another stage. They are typically stored in an S3 bucket during the pipeline run.
insight: Artifacts are the 'hand-offs' between different stages of your pipeline.

title: In-Place Deployment
content: The application on each instance in the deployment group is stopped, the latest application revision is installed, and the new version of the application is started.
insight: Fastest but involves downtime during the update.

title: Blue/Green Deployment
content: The new version (green) is deployed to a new set of instances. Once tested, traffic is shifted from the old version (blue) to the new version.
insight: Zero downtime and easy rollback if the new version fails.

title: Canary Deployment
content: Traffic is shifted to the new version in small increments (e.g., 10%, then 50%, then 100%). This allows you to monitor the new version with a small percentage of users.
insight: The safest way to roll out changes to a large user base.

title: Terraform in CI/CD
content: Just like application code, your Terraform code should be in a pipeline. The pipeline runs 'terraform plan' on pull requests and 'terraform apply' on merges.
insight: Automate your infrastructure changes for total consistency.

title: OIDC (OpenID Connect)
content: OIDC allows your GitHub Actions workflows to access AWS resources without needing long-lived AWS access keys. It uses short-lived tokens for better security.
insight: The modern, 'keyless' way to connect your CI/CD to the cloud.

title: Build Caching
content: Caching dependencies (like node_modules) between pipeline runs can significantly speed up your builds by avoiding redundant downloads.
insight: Save time and bandwidth by remembering what you've already built.

title: Docker Build in Pipeline
content: The pipeline builds a Docker image, tags it with the commit hash, and pushes it to a registry like ECR. This image is then used for deployment.
insight: Images are the perfect, immutable artifacts for cloud pipelines.

title: Linting and Static Analysis
content: Tools like ESLint (for JS) or TFLint (for Terraform) run in the pipeline to check code style and detect potential errors without running the code.
insight: Catch 'silly' mistakes before they ever reach a test server.

title: SCA (Software Composition Analysis)
content: Tools like Snyk or GitHub Dependency Review scan your dependencies for known vulnerabilities in the CI/CD pipeline.
insight: Don't let insecure third-party libraries compromise your cloud apps.

title: Quality Gates
content: A quality gate is a condition that must be met for the pipeline to continue (e.g., 'Unit test coverage must be > 80%').
insight: Enforce high standards automatically with every commit.

title: Manual Approval Actions
content: In some environments (like Production), you may want the pipeline to stop and wait for a human to review the changes before proceeding with the deployment.
insight: Combine automation with human oversight where it matters most.

title: Environment Variables
content: Pipelines use environment variables to configure different settings for different environments (e.g., DB_URL for Dev vs. DB_URL for Prod).
insight: Same code, different configurations for different stages.

title: Automated Rollback
content: If a deployment fails (detected by health checks or CloudWatch alarms), the pipeline should automatically roll back to the previous stable version.
insight: Failure happens; being able to recover instantly is what matters.

title: Drift Detection Pipelines
content: You can schedule a pipeline to run 'terraform plan' daily to detect if anyone has made manual changes to your infrastructure.
insight: Ensure your code and your cloud are always in sync.

title: What is GitOps?
content: GitOps is a set of practices that uses Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications. Tools like ArgoCD or Flux are commonly used.
insight: In GitOps, the 'actual state' of the cloud automatically pulls from the 'desired state' in Git.

title: ArgoCD and Kubernetes
content: ArgoCD is a declarative, GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes. it monitors your Git repo and automatically applies changes to your cluster.
insight: Keep your Kubernetes cluster perfectly in sync with your Git manifests.

title: Pipeline-as-Code
content: Treating your pipeline definition (YAML) as code means it is versioned, reviewed, and stored alongside your application code.
insight: The 'how we deploy' is just as important as the 'what we deploy'.

title: Internal Developer Platforms
content: Advanced CI/CD setups allow developers to provision their own 'ephemeral' environments for testing just by creating a pull request.
insight: Empower developers to move fast without waiting for Ops teams.

title: Global Pipelines
content: For global applications, a single pipeline can deploy your application to multiple cloud regions sequentially, ensuring a consistent global rollout.
insight: Deploy once, run everywhere (around the world).

title: Compliance Scanning
content: Pipelines can run compliance checks (e.g., using 'checkov' or 'tfsec') to ensure your infrastructure meets regulatory standards before it's deployed.
insight: Security and compliance are built into the delivery process.

title: Infracost
content: Infracost is a tool that runs in your CI/CD pipeline to show you exactly how much your infrastructure costs will change before you merge a pull request.
insight: See the bill before you build.

title: Conclusion
content: CI/CD is the engine that drives cloud speed and reliability. By automating the build, test, and deploy process, you can focus on writing great code while the pipeline handles the rest.
insight: A great cloud engineer is an expert at building great pipelines.
